name: Shakespeare
description: Timeless lines from the Bard's plays and sonnets, still echoing four centuries on.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: To be, or not to be, that is the question.
quotes.notes: Hamlet opens his most famous soliloquy as he contemplates life and death.

quotes.content: This above all: to thine own self be true.
quotes.notes: Polonius offers parting advice to his son Laertes.

quotes.content: Brevity is the soul of wit.
quotes.notes: Polonius says this to the King and Queen, ironically before a long-winded speech.

quotes.content: Though this be madness, yet there is method in't.
quotes.notes: Polonius, aside, observing Hamlet's seemingly mad behavior.

quotes.content: The lady doth protest too much, methinks.
quotes.notes: Queen Gertrude reacts to the player queen during the play-within-a-play; often misquoted with the word order reversed.

quotes.content: There is nothing either good or bad, but thinking makes it so.
quotes.notes: Hamlet to Rosencrantz and Guildenstern, on Denmark as a prison.

quotes.content: Something is rotten in the state of Denmark.
quotes.notes: Spoken by the guard Marcellus as Hamlet follows his father's ghost.

quotes.content: Alas, poor Yorick! I knew him, Horatio.
quotes.notes: Hamlet holds the skull of the former court jester in the graveyard scene.

quotes.content: The rest is silence.
quotes.notes: Hamlet's dying words.

quotes.content: Double, double toil and trouble; Fire burn and cauldron bubble.
quotes.notes: The three witches chant over their cauldron; commonly misremembered as 'bubble bubble.'

quotes.content: Out, damned spot! out, I say!
quotes.notes: Lady Macbeth, sleepwalking, imagines bloodstains she cannot wash away.

quotes.content: Is this a dagger which I see before me, the handle toward my hand?
quotes.notes: Macbeth hallucinates a dagger leading him to murder King Duncan.

quotes.content: Fair is foul, and foul is fair.
quotes.notes: The witches chant this in the play's opening scene, setting its theme of moral inversion.

quotes.content: Tomorrow, and tomorrow, and tomorrow, creeps in this petty pace from day to day.
quotes.notes: Macbeth's despairing soliloquy on hearing of his wife's death.

quotes.content: Life's but a walking shadow, a poor player that struts and frets his hour upon the stage and then is heard no more.
quotes.notes: Macbeth reflects on the futility of life moments before his end.

quotes.content: O Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou Romeo?
quotes.notes: Juliet at her window asks why Romeo must be a Montague ('wherefore' means 'why').

quotes.content: But soft, what light through yonder window breaks? It is the east, and Juliet is the sun.
quotes.notes: Romeo sees Juliet appear at her window during the balcony scene.

quotes.content: What's in a name? That which we call a rose by any other word would smell as sweet.
quotes.notes: Juliet argues that names are arbitrary; the Folger and Quarto texts read 'word,' often modernized to 'name.'

quotes.content: Parting is such sweet sorrow.
quotes.notes: Juliet bids Romeo good night at the close of the balcony scene.

quotes.content: A plague o' both your houses!
quotes.notes: Mercutio curses the Montagues and Capulets as he dies of his wound.

quotes.content: For never was a story of more woe than this of Juliet and her Romeo.
quotes.notes: The Prince's closing couplet that ends the play.

quotes.content: Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears.
quotes.notes: Mark Antony opens his funeral oration over Caesar's body.

quotes.content: Et tu, Brute?
quotes.notes: Caesar's last words on seeing Brutus among his assassins ('You too, Brutus?').

quotes.content: Cowards die many times before their deaths; the valiant never taste of death but once.
quotes.notes: Caesar dismisses his wife Calphurnia's fears on the morning of his death.

quotes.content: The fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars, but in ourselves, that we are underlings.
quotes.notes: Cassius urges Brutus to take action against Caesar rather than blaming fate.

quotes.content: Cry 'Havoc,' and let slip the dogs of war.
quotes.notes: Antony, alone with Caesar's corpse, prophesies the civil war to come.

quotes.content: Beware the ides of March.
quotes.notes: A soothsayer warns Caesar of the day he will be assassinated.

quotes.content: We are such stuff as dreams are made on, and our little life is rounded with a sleep.
quotes.notes: Prospero reflects on the illusory nature of life; note 'made on,' not 'made of.'

quotes.content: O brave new world, that has such people in't!
quotes.notes: Miranda marvels at the strangers she has never seen before.

quotes.content: Hell is empty, and all the devils are here.
quotes.notes: Ariel reports the words of the panicked passengers during the shipwreck.

quotes.content: All the world's a stage, and all the men and women merely players.
quotes.source: As You Like It, Act 2, Scene 7 (c. 1599)
quotes.notes: The melancholy Jaques begins his 'seven ages of man' speech.

quotes.content: The fool doth think he is wise, but the wise man knows himself to be a fool.
quotes.source: As You Like It, Act 5, Scene 1 (c. 1599)
quotes.notes: Spoken by the clown Touchstone.

quotes.content: If music be the food of love, play on.
quotes.notes: Duke Orsino's opening line, lovesick for Olivia.

quotes.content: Some are born great, some achieve greatness, and some have greatness thrust upon 'em.
quotes.notes: Malvolio reads aloud from the forged letter he believes is from Olivia.

quotes.content: Nothing will come of nothing.
quotes.notes: Lear warns Cordelia when she refuses to flatter him.

quotes.content: How sharper than a serpent's tooth it is to have a thankless child!
quotes.notes: Lear laments the ingratitude of his daughter Goneril.

quotes.content: As flies to wanton boys are we to th' gods; they kill us for their sport.
quotes.notes: The blinded Gloucester despairs of the cruelty of the gods.

quotes.content: Once more unto the breach, dear friends, once more.
quotes.notes: King Henry rallies his troops at the siege of Harfleur.

quotes.content: We few, we happy few, we band of brothers.
quotes.notes: King Henry inspires his outnumbered men in the St. Crispin's Day speech before Agincourt.

quotes.content: The quality of mercy is not strained; it droppeth as the gentle rain from heaven upon the place beneath.
quotes.source: The Merchant of Venice, Act 4, Scene 1 (c. 1596)
quotes.notes: Portia, disguised as a lawyer, pleads for mercy in the courtroom.

quotes.content: If you prick us, do we not bleed? If you tickle us, do we not laugh?
quotes.source: The Merchant of Venice, Act 3, Scene 1 (c. 1596)
quotes.notes: Shylock argues for the shared humanity of Jews and Christians.

quotes.content: All that glisters is not gold.
quotes.source: The Merchant of Venice, Act 2, Scene 7 (c. 1596)
quotes.notes: The Prince of Morocco reads this scroll inside the golden casket; 'glisters,' not 'glitters.'

quotes.content: The course of true love never did run smooth.
quotes.source: A Midsummer Night's Dream, Act 1, Scene 1 (c. 1595)
quotes.notes: Lysander consoles Hermia about the obstacles to their love.

quotes.content: Lord, what fools these mortals be!
quotes.source: A Midsummer Night's Dream, Act 3, Scene 2 (c. 1595)
quotes.notes: Puck remarks to Oberon on the lovers' confusion.

quotes.content: And though she be but little, she is fierce.
quotes.source: A Midsummer Night's Dream, Act 3, Scene 2 (c. 1595)
quotes.notes: Helena describes the small but formidable Hermia.

quotes.content: Now is the winter of our discontent made glorious summer by this sun of York.
quotes.notes: Richard, Duke of Gloucester, opens the play with this soliloquy.

quotes.content: A horse! a horse! my kingdom for a horse!
quotes.notes: King Richard cries out, unhorsed, at the Battle of Bosworth Field.

quotes.content: Uneasy lies the head that wears a crown.
quotes.notes: The sleepless King Henry IV reflects on the burdens of kingship.

quotes.content: The better part of valour is discretion.
quotes.notes: Falstaff justifies playing dead to survive the battle.

quotes.content: O, beware, my lord, of jealousy; it is the green-eyed monster which doth mock the meat it feeds on.
quotes.notes: Iago slyly plants suspicion in Othello's mind.

quotes.content: Shall I compare thee to a summer's day? Thou art more lovely and more temperate.
quotes.notes: The opening lines of one of Shakespeare's best-known sonnets.

quotes.content: Let me not to the marriage of true minds admit impediments. Love is not love which alters when it alteration finds.
quotes.notes: A sonnet defining constant, unchanging love.

quotes.content: My mistress' eyes are nothing like the sun.
quotes.notes: The opening line of a sonnet that mocks the clichés of love poetry.

quotes.content: When in disgrace with fortune and men's eyes, I all alone beweep my outcast state.
quotes.notes: The opening lines of a sonnet that turns from despair to gratitude for love.
